speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Based on your recent stress test and angiogram, I’m confirming a diagnosis of Coronary Artery Disease, dated February 25th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your resting heart rate today is 62 beats per minute — that’s within the normal range and reassuring for someone with CAD.

speaker: doctor
text: I see you're currently on Metformin 1000 mg twice daily — we’ll keep that going for your type 2 diabetes management, as it also has cardiovascular benefits.

speaker: patient
text: Thank you, Doctor. I’ve been trying to walk more and cut back on salt, but I’ve had some chest tightness when climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — let’s discuss those symptoms in more detail. Any shortness of breath or dizziness accompanying the tightness?

speaker: patient
text: Just mild shortness of breath, no dizziness. It usually eases after I stop and rest for a minute or two.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level came back at 180 g/L — that’s elevated, though not critically so. We’ll monitor this closely, especially given your CAD.

speaker: doctor
text: Have you noticed any headaches, flushing, or unusual fatigue lately? Those can sometimes accompany higher hemoglobin levels.

speaker: patient
text: A bit more fatigue than usual, and yes — occasional headaches, especially in the morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure reading today is 165 mmHg. That’s above our target for CAD management, which is ideally below 130 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been checking it at home — it’s usually around 155–160 in the evening. I take my medications regularly.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for tracking that. Let’s recheck it now — please sit quietly for a minute before we measure again.

speaker: doctor
text: Hmm — this reading shows 180 mmHg systolic. That’s notably higher than the 165 we saw earlier. Was there anything different just before this measurement?

speaker: patient
text: I stood up quickly before the cuff went on — maybe that spiked it? I did feel a little lightheaded for a second.

speaker: doctor
text: That could explain the elevation. Still, even accounting for that, your BP remains suboptimally controlled, and we need to address it proactively.

speaker: doctor
text: Interestingly, your repeat hemoglobin value is again 180 g/L — consistent with the earlier lab draw. This reinforces that the elevation isn’t a fluke.

speaker: patient
text: Does that mean I might have polycythemia? My brother was diagnosed with something similar last year.

speaker: doctor
text: It’s possible, but we’ll need additional tests — like serum erythropoietin and JAK2 mutation testing — to clarify. For now, let’s focus on your BP and cardiac risk reduction.

speaker: doctor
text: I am going to switch you from your current antihypertensive regimen to Lisinopril 20 mg once daily — it’s an ACE inhibitor with strong evidence for reducing cardiovascular events in CAD patients.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — and just to confirm, that hemoglobin number you mentioned earlier — the 180 g/L — is the same one from the lab report you showed me at the start?
